macro_segments.title: The Distinction Between Right Horn and Left Horn in the Doctrine of Shemira (Supervision)

micro_segments.title: Introduction to Rav's Teaching on Right and Left Horns
The Gemara's Question: According to Which Authority Does Rav's Distinction Make Sense?
According to Rabbi Meir: Both Tam and Muad Require High Shemira
According to Rabbi Yehuda: The Clever Case of Muad Right and Tam Left
The Gemara's Objection: Rabbi Yehuda's Position Works Without Even Introducing Two Horns
Resolution: Rav Must Be Following Rabbi Yehuda's Position

macro_segments.title: Rav's Rejection of the Doctrine of Tzad Tamut Be-Mekoma Omedes (The Tam Aspect Remains in Place)

micro_segments.title: Rav's Response to Rav Adda bar Ahava on the Half-Muad Half-Tam Status
Rav's Argument: A Fully Muad Animal Has No Tam Aspect; Right and Left Horns Are the Only Exception

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer's Position: Only Ritual Slaughter Exempts from Liability for a Muad Ox

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer's Statement and Its Meaning: No Shemira Except the Knife
Rava's Explanation: The Verse 'And He Did Not Watch It' Implies Impossibility
Abaye's Challenge: The Same Logic Should Apply to a Pit—But It Does Not
The Source: Rabbi Natan's Teaching on Hazards in One's Home
Rashi's Explanation: The Obligation to Remove Creates the Liability
Tosafot's First Answer: A Living Ox Cannot Be Neutralized Like a Covered Pit
Tosafot's Second Answer: Civil Liability Versus Religious Obligation

macro_segments.title: New Perek: An Ox Gores a Pregnant Cow—Uncertainty About Timing of Birth

micro_segments.title: The Scenario: Gored Cow Found with Dead Fetus; Unclear If Birth Preceded Goring
The Mishnah's Solution: Half Damages for Cow, Quarter Damages for Fetus
The Principle of Rav Yehuda: This Follows Sumchus—Money Suspended in Doubt Is Split
The Chachamim's Objection: Hamotzi Mechabero—The Burden of Proof

macro_segments.title: The Principle of Hamotzi Mechabero Alav Haraya (Burden of Proof) in Various Contexts

micro_segments.title: General Statement of the Principle and Its Scope
The Principle Applies Even in Cases of Bari (Certainty) Versus Shema (Doubt)
Monetary Matters: Hamotzi Applies Rather Than Following Rov (Majority)
Source of the Principle from the Verse 'Mi Ba'al Devarim'
Rav Ashi's Challenge: The Principle Derives from Sevara Alone, Not a Verse
Application to Order of Litigation: Suit Before Countersuit

macro_segments.title: The Case of a Cow Goring an Ox—Reverse Scenario with Separate Ownership

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah's Parallel Case: Pregnant Cow Gores Ox; Same Uncertainty About Timing
The Apparent Problem: Why Half and Quarter, Not Full from the Cow?
Abaye's Resolution: Half Means One-Quarter of Damages; Quarter Means One-Eighth
If Cow and Fetus Have Same Owner: No Need to Split; Collect Fully from the Cow
The Logic: Treating Cow and Fetus as Equal Gorers When Fetus Is Separate Property
If Separate Owners: Analyze Whether You Sue Cow Owner or Fetus Owner First
Gemara's Discussion: Can the Cow Owner Avoid Full Payment If the Fetus Owner Was Sued First?
